entries.falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
entries.mechanism: A rising order backlog and scarce high-bandwidth memory mean NVIDIA still cannot ship enough GPUs to fill the demand buyers are placing.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast high-bandwidth memory and advanced-packaging capacity catch up to order volume.
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.title: NVDA demand durability

entries.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
entries.mechanism: High-bandwidth memory and packaging capacity, not silicon wafers, set the ceiling on how many GPUs NVIDIA and AMD can build and sell.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory-maker capacity additions from Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung.
entries.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
entries.title: Supply constraint binding

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
entries.mechanism: Named hyperscaler wins and 57% data-center revenue growth show AMD is taking a growing slice of accelerator spending.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether MI450 and Helios ramp on schedule in H2 2026 and to ROCm software maturity.
entries.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
entries.title: AMD credible second source

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
entries.mechanism: Hyperscalers keep buying more merchant GPUs even as their own Trainium and TPU chips reach mass production, so in-house silicon is not eating GPU demand growth.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the share of new hyperscaler capacity that goes to in-house accelerators rather than merchant GPUs.
entries.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
entries.title: Custom ASIC substitution

entries.falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
entries.mechanism: Scarce supply keeps hourly rental rates for H100 and B200 well above prior floors even as new capacity comes online.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the pace at which new Blackwell and next-gen supply floods the rental market.
entries.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
entries.title: Pricing power persistence

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: U.S. licensing rules and a 25% import tariff drove NVIDIA's China data-center compute sales from billions to zero, removing a large block of addressable demand.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether case-by-case licenses convert into shipped, tariff-cleared China revenue.
entries.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
entries.title: Export control exposure

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA borrowed 25 billion dollars and gives startups GPUs in exchange for future revenue shares, so part of reported demand rests on financing NVIDIA itself provides.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether financed partners like Sharon AI and Firmus convert their commitments into paid cash revenue.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
entries.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
entries.mechanism: A handful of large buyers, including financed partners running over 200,000 GPUs each, still account for most of NVIDIA's data-center sales.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether NVIDIA's push into many smaller neocloud and startup buyers actually broadens its revenue base.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
entries.title: Customer concentration risk

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA sells Spectrum-X and Quantum switches together with Blackwell and GB300 racks, so buyers who want the full system take NVIDIA networking too.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether hyperscalers adopt open Ultra Ethernet fabrics instead of NVIDIA's bundled networking.
entries.statement: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
entries.title: Networking attach as systems moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Anthropic and others put production inference on NVIDIA's newest GB300 GPUs because each generation lowers the cost of serving a token.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether cheaper per-token inference on rival TPU and Trainium chips pulls workloads away.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
entries.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Buyers have prepaid about 18 billion dollars in deposits under non-cancelable five-year memory contracts, so much of future accelerator demand is contractually committed rather than merely forecast.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether AI-cloud buyers can fund and absorb the prepaid memory volumes they have committed to.
entries.statement: Non-cancelable multi-year HBM contracts backed by billions in customer deposits lock a large share of future accelerator demand into place and reduce the chance that today's buildout is speculative.
entries.title: Prepaid HBM take-or-pay locks in accelerator demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
entries.mechanism: Case-by-case licenses let some China orders through, yet the 25% tariff and the cap limiting China shipments to under half of matching U.S. sales hold realized China revenue far below the prior peak.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether case-by-case licenses convert into shipped, tariff-cleared China revenue rather than staying on paper.
entries.statement: U.S. regulators now allow case-by-case China sales of H200 and MI325X-class chips, but a 25% tariff, security conditions, and a volume cap keep any China revenue recovery small.
entries.title: Conditional China re-opening with a capped ceiling
